articles.title: Delhi enforces heatwave plan as temperatures rise above 40°C for weeks - The News Mill
articles.full_text:
Delhi Chief Minister Rekha Gupta has instructed officials to strictly enforce the Heat Wave Action Plan 2026 in response to intensifying heatwave conditions.
She warned of a disturbing pattern in the capital where temperatures have surpassed 40°C for almost 40 consecutive days over the past two to three years. This year, the government conducted a detailed scientific survey of the city using satellite data to pinpoint high-risk heat zones.
Among the most vulnerable locations is Ayanagar in South Delhi, which has recorded temperatures as high as 45.5°C. Other areas with extreme highs include Najafgarh (43.7°C in 2025) and Safdarjung (46.8°C in 2023). Additional emerging heat hotspots identified are Wazirpur, Jahangirpuri, Khayala, Shastri Park, Vishwas Nagar, Harkesh Nagar, Hari Nagar, and Delhi Gate.
Densely populated fringe areas such as Sawda, Mubarakpur Dabas, Bhalswa, Nand Nagri, Gokulpuri, and Bakkarwala are experiencing significant ‘heat island’ effects, prompting special protective measures. These measures include increased stocks of oral rehydration salts (ORS) at health centres, deployment of Quick Response Teams, and provision of additional water tankers.
The Chief Minister instructed departments, notably the DDA, Education Department, and Delhi Jal Board, to provide water and shade for people, animals, and birds. Water containers for birds and dedicated points for stray animals are being installed in parks, bus depots, and school campuses.
To protect schoolchildren, the government may administer ORS solutions before their departure from schools to mitigate dehydration risks during commutes. Strict protection measures for construction workers during peak heat hours include suspending outdoor work between 12 noon and 3 pm under severe heatwave conditions; providing drinking water, caps, and Gamchas; and ensuring first-aid kits and ice packs are accessible at worksites.
Delhi is advancing its ‘Cool Roof Policy 2026’, having already applied reflective coatings over approximately 28,674 square feet at the Kashmere Gate ISBT to reduce indoor temperatures. High-pressure misting systems will be installed at bus stops, and anti-smog guns will cool densely built areas.
The Health Department has placed over 339 health centres across 13 districts on alert. More than 30 hospitals have established dedicated five-bed ‘cool rooms’ for heatwave patients. Residents can access assistance via 24×7 helplines 1077, 1070, or 112. Additionally, 39 Quick Response Teams and trained ASHA workers remain on standby. Cold drinking water and ORS will be provided at busy public places including bus stops and terminals.
Chief Minister Gupta emphasised the government’s commitment to maintaining power supply amid summer demand, predicted to exceed last year’s peak of 8,442 MW by reaching over 9,000 MW. She stated, “We have issued strict directives to the power distribution companies, making it clear that any cuts in supply will not be tolerated.” A special priority protocol ensures uninterrupted 24×7 power to critical infrastructure such as hospitals, Water Treatment Plants, and mobile towers.
Power companies have been instructed to deploy additional Quick Response Teams and mobile transformers to promptly address transformer issues and technical faults, ensuring swift restoration of supply in emergencies.
